author: Justin Martyr
interpretation: Justin reads Psalm 22 as a direct prophecy of Christ's crucifixion, arguing that the details — pierced hands and feet, casting lots for garments, being surrounded by mockers — could not apply to David or any other figure. He presses this reading against Trypho the Jew, insisting that no event in David's life matches the psalm's specifics.
context: Justin's Dialogue is the earliest surviving Christian-Jewish debate text. His christological reading of Psalm 22 set the template for all subsequent Christian apologetic use of this psalm.

author: Origen of Alexandria
interpretation: Origen reads the opening cry as Christ speaking in his human nature — the divine Logos cannot suffer or be forsaken, but the assumed humanity genuinely experiences abandonment. The psalm thus demonstrates the reality of the incarnation: Christ truly entered human suffering, not as mere appearance but as lived experience.
context: Origen's two-natures reading of the cry of dereliction anticipated the Chalcedonian formula by two centuries and influenced how later theologians handled the apparent contradiction of God forsaking God.

author: John Chrysostom
interpretation: Chrysostom argues that Christ quotes Psalm 22:1 from the cross to direct listeners to the entire psalm — including its triumphant conclusion where God vindicates the sufferer and the nations worship. The cry of abandonment is not despair but a hermeneutical pointer: read the whole psalm and you will see both the suffering and the vindication that follows.
context: Chrysostom's pastoral reading resolved the troubling cry by embedding it in the psalm's larger arc of deliverance. This reading remains popular in preaching and devotional literature.

author: Thomas Aquinas
interpretation: Aquinas distinguishes between Christ's higher will (which remained united to the Father) and his lower, sensible will (which experienced genuine suffering and desolation). The cry of forsakenness expresses the real experience of the human nature without implying any rupture in the Trinity. Christ's suffering was maximal precisely because his capacity for both joy and pain exceeded all other humans.
context: Aquinas's faculty psychology allowed him to affirm both the reality of Christ's suffering and the impossibility of intra-Trinitarian rupture — a distinction that continues to shape Catholic and some Protestant Christology.

author: Rashi (Rabbi Shlomo Yitzchaki)
interpretation: Rashi reads Psalm 22 as David's own prayer during persecution, or alternatively as a prophetic voice for Israel in exile. The 'piercing' language in verse 16 is translated as 'like a lion' rather than 'they pierced,' following the Masoretic vocalization. The psalm describes corporate Jewish suffering under oppression, not an individual messianic figure.
context: Rashi's reading represents the mainstream Jewish interpretive tradition and directly counters the Christian christological reading. The textual dispute over verse 16 (ka'ari vs. ka'aru) remains one of the most debated translation questions in biblical studies.

author: John Calvin
interpretation: Calvin insists that the psalm's primary sense is David's own experience of suffering and apparent abandonment by God. However, David's experience was a type that found its ultimate fulfillment in Christ. Calvin reads the cry as genuine anguish — Christ truly felt forsaken, experiencing the curse of sin on behalf of humanity. This was not pretense but real spiritual torment.
context: Calvin's willingness to take the cry of forsakenness at face value — as real divine abandonment experienced by Christ's human consciousness — was more radical than most patristic readings and influenced Reformed atonement theology.

author: Franz Delitzsch
interpretation: Delitzsch reads Psalm 22 as directly messianic — not merely typologically but prophetically. David, speaking by the Spirit, described sufferings that transcended his own experience and could only be fulfilled in the Messiah. Delitzsch meticulously catalogs the correspondences between the psalm's details and the Gospel passion narratives, arguing that coincidence is insufficient to explain them.
context: Delitzsch was a conservative Lutheran scholar who engaged critically with the emerging historical-critical method while defending traditional messianic readings. His Psalms commentary remains a standard conservative reference.

author: Darrell Bock
interpretation: Bock reads Psalm 22 through the lens of typological-prophetic fulfillment. David's experience of suffering and vindication establishes a pattern that Jesus consciously invokes and fulfills. By quoting the psalm's opening line, Jesus identifies himself as the ultimate righteous sufferer and signals that the psalm's conclusion — universal worship — will be accomplished through his death and resurrection.
context: Bock's work on how the New Testament uses the Old Testament has shaped evangelical hermeneutics. His pattern-fulfillment model mediates between pure prediction and pure typology.
